items.q: What did Chomsky propose about language acquisition?
items.correct: Children possess an innate Language Acquisition Device — an inborn capacity for grammar — because exposure alone cannot account for how rapidly and uniformly children learn complex syntax.
items.misconception: Chomsky said children learn language entirely by copying adults.
items.random_wrong: Chomsky said children should be taught grammar from age two.
items.why: Chomsky's theory is precisely the opposite of behaviourist imitation. Students confuse Chomsky with Skinner — Chomsky's argument is that imitation cannot explain acquisition.

items.q: What is the difference between transitive and intransitive verbs?
items.correct: Transitive verbs take a direct object ('she ate the cake'); intransitive verbs don't ('she slept'). Some verbs are both depending on use ('she ran' / 'she ran the company').
items.misconception: Transitive verbs are action verbs and intransitive are linking verbs.
items.random_wrong: Transitive verbs end in -ed and intransitive in -ing.
items.why: Transitivity is about object-taking, not action vs state. Both transitive and intransitive verbs can be action verbs. Students conflate transitivity with verb category, missing the syntactic criterion.

items.q: How does Piaget's theory relate language to cognitive development?
items.correct: Language develops as a consequence of cognitive development — children must first develop concepts (object permanence, conservation) before they can express them linguistically. Cognition leads language.
items.misconception: Piaget said language drives cognitive development.
items.random_wrong: Piaget was a behaviourist who emphasised reinforcement.
items.why: Piaget's order is cognition first, then language — opposite of Whorfian linguistic determinism. Vygotsky disagreed, arguing language and thought interact. Students confuse the direction of Piaget's claim.
It's Pronunciation. “Vocabulary” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
It's The widely accepted shared form. “The poshest possible English” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many concepts listed (Standard English, dialect, accent, slang, colloquial, register)?
items.why: The real figure is 6. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
